column6: Differentially expressed genes in mouse nucleus accumbens (NAc) SAL-SAL vs. SAL-NLX (control) RNAseq_log2FC
column7: DEG mouse NAc control_log2FC
column9: Male and female C57BL/6J mice, mu opioid receptor (Oprm1) knockout mice, and dopamine transporter (DAT)-IRES-Cre knock-in mice were obtained from The Jackson Laboratory or bred in-house. Mice were 6-12 weeks old at the beginning of each experiment. Osmotic minipumps (Alzet Model 2001) were implanted in mice weighing up to 25g, which is the upper limit for administration of 63.2 mg/kg/day morphine using these minipumps. After adjusting morphine concentration for body weight, minipumps were filled with 300 μL of solution and primed overnight at 40°C. Miniaturized programmable infusion pumps (iPrecio SMP-300) were implanted in mice weighing at least 20g. The pump reservoir was filled with saline (SAL) or morphine (MOR) (~50 mg/mL) according to manufacturer’s instructions, and then wirelessly programmed to infuse with one of two patterns: (1) a continuous pattern with sustained infusion for 7 consecutive days, or (2) a “discontinuous” pattern with alternating 24-hour periods of drug infusion and pump inactivity for 13 days. To interrupt continuous morphine infusion while controlling pharmacokinetic variables, we administered two daily naloxone (NLX) injections separated by an interval of 2h (Fig. 2a), as previously described. We selected a high dose of naloxone (10mg/kg) to fully interrupt activation of opioid receptors by morphine, as pilot studies showed this naloxone dose had more robust effects than lower doses. We assessed the downstream consequences of continuous and interrupted morphine exposure on gene expression in the nucleus accumbens. To minimize variability related to sex differences, we used only male mice in this experiment, since interrupted morphine caused more robust locomotor sensitization in males (n=5–6 male mice/group). At the end of chronic treatment, we dissected the nucleus accumbens as well as the dorsal striatum (Fig. 4a), and used next-generation RNA sequencing to perform genome-wide transcriptional profiling. Truseq libraries were sequenced 50-bp paired-end run on the Illumina HiSeq 2500, generating roughly 20 million paired-end reads per run. Cleaned reads were aligned to the Mus musculus reference genome, version GRCm38 with HISAT2. Nucleus accumbens and dorsal striatum samples were handled separately for all analyses. For differential expression analysis, filtered expression counts were normalized and variance-stabilized with DESeq2. One sample from the nucleus accumbens was identified as an outlier based on both PCA and hierarchical clustering, and was removed from further analyses. We defined differential gene expression with a fold change threshold of 15%, while controlling false discovery rate at q<0.05. From supplementary table S3.

column6: Genes associated with significantly differentially expressed circRNA (microarray) in rat orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) heroin (0.03 mg/kg) vs saline self-administration_pvalue
column7: Sig. DE gene-derived circRNA rat OFC heroin vs saline_pvalue
column9: Regulation of a novel class of RNAs, circular RNAs (circRNAs), by the addictive opioid heroin in the rat orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) was explored. Seventy-seven adult male and fifty-six adult female Sprague Dawley rats (Charles River Laboratories), 8 weeks old, were used in this study. Heroin self-administration studies were conducted in operant chambers under a fixed ratio (FR) 1 schedule. For heroin self-administration, active lever pressing resulted in activation of the infusion pump for intravenous infusion of 0.03 mg/kg/infusion of heroin solution, presentation of a 65 db, 2.9 kHz acoustic cue and illumination of a stimulus light above the active lever. A 20-s time out period followed each drug infusion. Animals were trained for 10 days in 6 h daily sessions. A separate group of rats underwent saline self-administration and did not receive heroin infusions. Immediately after the 10th self-administration session, all animals were briefly anesthetized with 5% isoflurane and rapidly decapitated. Total RNA was extracted from OFC tissue using the MIRvana Paris Protein & RNA Isolation System (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Detection of differentially expressed circRNAs was achieved with microarray analyses performed by Arraystar Inc. (Rockville, MD) (082778 Arraystar Rat CircRNA microarray V1). A total of 2.5 μg of pure RNA from 8 individual male OFC samples was submitted for analysis. Statistical significance was estimated by t-test. circRNAs having fold changes >1.5 and p-values < 0.05 were selected as significantly differentially expressed. From supplementary table 1.

column6: Significant differential protein expression in rat hippocampus (HIP) 24hr after 10-day increasing morhine exposure (10–40 mg/kg) (+M10) vs control (-M10)_pvalue
column7: Sig. differential protein expression rat HIP 24hr after 10-day morphine (+M10) vs control (-M10)_pvalue
column9: MALDI-TOF MS/MS analysis of eight altered protein spots (within 6 protein-coding genes; this geneset) in post-nuclear supernatant (PNS) prepared from hippocampus of rats exposed to morphine for 10 days and sacrificed 24 h after the last dose; difference of protein composition in PNS samples prepared from groups (+M10) (morphine) and (−M10) (control). Young adult morphine-naive male Wistar rats (220–250 g) were exposed to increasing doses of morphine (10–40 mg/kg) dissolved in 0.9% NaCl for 10 consecutive days as described before. Control animals received 0.9% NaCl for 10 days and were sacrificed in parallel with morphine-treated rats. The two-dimensional fluorescence difference gel electrophoresis (2D-DIGE) and matrix assisted laser desorption ionization-time of flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS/MS) was used for comparison of protein profiling in hippocampal samples. Lowry method was used for determination of protein concentration in all hippocampal samples of PNS (+M10, −M10, +M10/−M20 and −M10/−M20) using bovine serum albumin (Sigma, Fraction V) as a standard. Data were calculated by fitting the calibration curve as a quadratic equation. Protein levels showing significant quantitative differences at least 2-fold (p≤0.05) were selected for mass spectrometric analysis. The data were analyzed using in house running Mascot server 2.2.07 and matched against comprehensive UniProt database of protein sequences (27929 sequences; 14725510 residues). The DAVID gene ID conversion tool was used to convert UniProt accession IDs to Entrez IDs by GeneWeaver curator. For genes with multiple altered protein spots within them, only the most significant p-values are included (i.e. Entrez 24383). From Table 1.

column9: Subcellular localization and function of morphine-altered proteins in groups (+M10) (morphine) and (−M10) (control) identified by label-free quantification (MaxLFQ). Young adult morphine-naive male Wistar rats (220–250 g) were exposed to increasing doses of morphine (10–40 mg/kg) dissolved in 0.9% NaCl for 10 consecutive days and sacrificed 24 h after the last dose. Control animals received 0.9% NaCl for 10 days and were sacrificed in parallel with morphine-treated rats. The 200 μg of hippocampal PNS samples (+M10), (−M10), (+M10/−M20) and (−M10/−M20) were processed as described previously. All data were analyzed and quantified with MaxQuant software. The false discovery rate (FDR) was set to 1% for both proteins and peptides and a minimum length of seven amino acids was specified. The Andromeda search engine was used for the MS/MS spectra search against the UniProt Rattus norvegicus database of protein sequences. Only at least 1.7-fold significant differences calculated for at least 2 measured values from 3 replicates were taken into consideration. Lowry method was used for determination of protein concentration in all hippocampal samples of PNS (+M10, −M10, +M10/−M20 and −M10/−M20) using bovine serum albumin (Sigma, Fraction V) as a standard. Data were calculated by fitting the calibration curve as a quadratic equation. From Table 5.

Lowry method was used for determination of protein concentration in all hippocampal samples of PNS (+M10, −M10, +M10/−M20 and −M10/−M20) using bovine serum albumin (Sigma, Fraction V) as a standard. Data were calculated by fitting the calibration curve as a quadratic equation. From Table 6.

column6: Differential CpG methylation (5mC) nearest gene site in orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) in OUD cases vs controls_delta5mC
column7: Differential CpG methylation 5mC OFC OUD_delta5mC
column9: Postmortem human brain specimens were obtained from the National Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) Brain Bank (NPBB), a brain tissue repository at the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA). Brain tissue samples were collected from the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC; Brodmann Area 11) from non-OUD subjects (no history of OUD diagnosis) and those with OUD diagnosis who died from drug and/or alcohol intoxication not limited to opioids. Individual CpGs were evaluated for differential 5mC and 5hmC levels in individuals with OUD compared to those without OUD. Annotation of the closest Ensembl transcript ID of the differential sites was performed using the Genomation60 R package. The annotation considered any distance between the site and the transcription start site (TSS). Gene symbols were annotated using the biomaRt R package. In addition, a complimentary annotation was conducted using the UCSC genome browser to identify the gene region location. A threshold of 1500 bp was applied (“Position to the Nearest gene ID” in Supplementary Data 1 and 2). Differential analyses were performed using methylkit, which applies logistic regression to calculate the p-values. From supplementary Data 1. FDR adjusted p < 0.05. For gene sites that were nearest to multiple CpGs, the gene/CpG with the lowest pvalue is presented.

column6: Differentially expressed genes in nucleus accumbens (Nac) of acute morphine-treated vs saline KM mouse model_pvalue
column7: DEG mouse Nac acute morphine vs saline_pvalue
column9: Kunming (KM) mice (Fujian University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, China) were used in the experiments. Twelve 8-week-old male mice were used for the acute morphine treatment. The mice were randomly divided into two groups (morphine and saline). To remove interference caused by the abdominal injections, all the animals received injections of saline for 3 days. On day 4, the animals in the morphine group were injected with 10 mg·kg-1 morphine (about 300 μl), whereas the control animals received an equal volume of saline. The animals were euthanized by decapitation after 4 hours. The nucleus accumbens was dissected immediately, rapidly frozen in liquid nitrogen, and stored at -80°C until analysis. For mRNA-sequence (mRNA-seq) sample preparation, the Illumina standard kit was used according to the TruSeq RNA Sample Preparation Guide v2. Sequencing was performed on an Illumina HiSeq2000 sequencing system. Differentially expressed genes are defined as q-value < 0.05 by Cuffdiff v2.0.2 program (shown here). From Table S2.

column6: Differentially expressed genes in nucleus accumbens (Nac) of morphine-treated vs saline KM mouse CPP model_pvalue
column7: DEG mouse Nac CPP morphine vs saline_pvalue
column9: Kunming (KM) mice (Fujian University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, China) were used in the experiments. Nine 8-week-old male mice were used in conditioned place preference (CPP) experiments. The mice were randomly divided into two groups (morphine and saline). The CPP protocol consisted of a 10-day schedule with three distinct phases: preconditioning, conditioning, and postconditioning. Because of the study objective, a final stage, the reactivation of the memory trace, was included on day 11, 1 day after the place preference conditioning. The conditioning phase (from day 2 to day 9) consisted of eight 45-min sessions. The mice in the morphine group received an abdominal injection of 25 mg·kg-1 morphine on days 2, 4, 6, and 8 and the same volume of saline on days 3, 5, 7, and 9. The control mice received saline every day. The animals were euthanized 4 hours after the reactivation of the memory trace stage, and the nucleus accumbens was excised immediately, rapidly frozen in liquid nitrogen, and stored at -80°C until analysis. The nucleus accumbens was dissected immediately. For mRNA-sequence (mRNA-seq) sample preparation, the Illumina standard kit was used according to the TruSeq RNA Sample Preparation Guide v2 (Illumina). Sequencing was performed on an Illumina HiSeq2000 sequencing system. Differentially expressed genes are defined as q-value < 0.05 by Cuffdiff v2.0.2 program (shown here). From Table S3.

column6: Differential gene expression in female rat nucleus accumbens (Nac) after fentanyl- or saline-vs-food choice procedure followed by extended access to fentanyl or saline_log2FC
column7: DEG female rat NAc fentanyl vs saline_log2FC
column9: Male (n = 13) and female (n = 12) Sprague Dawley rats responded under a fentanyl-versus-food choice procedure during daily 2-hour sessions. A total of 8 rats (4 male, 4 female) were trained to respond under a saline-versus-food choice procedure. In addition to the daily choice sessions, rats were provided extended access to fentanyl or saline during 12-hour self-administration sessions. After 2 weeks of this self-administration regimen, the nucleus accumbens and ventral tegmental area of a subset of rats were subjected to RNA sequencing. Sequence reads were trimmed to remove possible adapter sequences and nucleotides with poor quality using Trimmomatic v.0.36. The trimmed reads were mapped to the Rattus norvegicus Rnor6.0 reference genome available on Ensembl using the STAR aligner v.2.5.2b. Unique gene hit counts were calculated by using featureCounts from the Subread package v.1.5.2. After extraction of gene hit counts, the gene hit counts table was used for downstream differential expression analysis. Using DESeq2, a comparison of gene expression between groups of samples was performed. The Wald test was used to generate p values and log2 fold changes. Genes with an adjusted p value < .05 and absolute log2 fold change >1 were designated as DEGs for each comparison. From supplementary data 2.

column6: Differentially expressed genes (mRNA) in mouse striatum associated with addiction to oxycodone vs saline_log2FC
column7: DEG (mRNA) mouse striatum oxycodone addiction_log2FC
column9: RNA‐seq was used to investigate the pathology of oxycodone abuse. In order to uncover the potential epitranscriptomic role of m6A methylation in oxycodone abuse, three different doses of saline or oxycodone (1.5, 3.0, and 6.0mg/kg) were administered intraperitoneally (i.p., 0.1mL/10g) to a group of mice for 9 days. Subsequently, after 4 days of oxycodone withdrawal and 1hr after open field testing, striatum samples from the experimental mice were collected for RNA‐sequencing (RNA‐seq) to detect m6A methylation‐associated enzymes and then examine m6A‐related epigenetic alterations. Total RNA was extracted from the striatum (n=3 C57BL/6J mice in each group), and the quantification of lncRNA, ncRNA, and mRNA followed. Quality‐checked libraries were sequenced on the DNBseq platform using MGISEQ‐2000 with 100PE sequencing. Library construction, lncRNA‐seq, ncRNA‐seq, and RNA‐seq, as well as data collection and mapping were outsourced to HuaDa Gene Biotechnology (Shenzhen, China). From supplementary table 1. Please note that the upregulated genes reported in the Results section correspond to negative fold change values in the table, and downregulated correspond to positive fold change values - this may or may not be an error in reporting by the authors.

column9: We explored whether differential methylation related to opioid use differed by sex using studies having more than 15 opioid users in both sex groups, which limited the meta-analysis to Agricultural Lung Health Study (ALHS) and Framingham Heart Study (FHS). ALHS participants, enrolled between 2009 and 2013, were asked at home visits to provide all prescription and nonprescription medications used in the last 7 days and field technicians recorded the medication names, which were coded using the Slone Drug Dictionary. Recent opioid use was classified based on any use of full opioid agonists in the past 7 days versus no use. In the FHS, participants were asked to bring all prescription and nonprescription medications taken regularly in the past month in their original containers to the study visit. Medication names were converted into Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical codes. Recent opioid use (users vs nonusers) was based on any use of full opioid agonists in the past month. We measured genome-wide methylation in blood, collected at the time of the medication questionnaires, using Illumina methylation arrays. FHS used the Illumina HumanMethylation450 BeadChip (450K array; Illumina, Inc., CA, USA) and the other studies used the newer MethylationEPIC BeadChip (EPIC/850K array; Illumina, Inc.). Gene names used were those listed on the Illumina annotation. When no gene names were reported on the Illumina annotation, the authors used Zhou et al. and Homer v4.9.1 to annotate genes. Data shown are for males that were opioid exposed (N = 83) vs opioid unexposed (N = 3,025). FDR < 0.05. From supplementary table 2.

column6: Significant differentially methylated regions (DMRs) related to opioid medication use in human blood (all participants)_pvalue
column7: Sig. DMRs opioid use human blood_pvalue
column9: This meta-analysis included 10,842 adults (≥30 years old) from five datasets, within the framework of the Cohorts for Heart and Aging Research in Genomic Epidemiology consortium. Participating studies included the Agricultural Lung Health Study (ALHS), Framingham Heart Study (FHS), Generation Scotland (GS Set1 and Set2), and Genetic Epidemiology Network of Arteriopathy (GENOA). Study participants were mainly of European ancestry (91% of the 10,842 adults). African-ancestry individuals were from GENOA. ALHS participants, enrolled between 2009 and 2013, were asked at home visits to provide all prescription and nonprescription medications used in the last 7 days and field technicians recorded the medication names, which were coded using the Slone Drug Dictionary. Recent opioid use was classified based on any use of full opioid agonists in the past 7 days versus no use. In the FHS, participants were asked to bring all prescription and nonprescription medications taken regularly in the past month in their original containers to the study visit. Medication names were converted into Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical codes. Recent opioid use (users vs nonusers) was based on any use of full opioid agonists in the past month. We measured genome-wide methylation in blood, collected at the time of the medication questionnaires, using Illumina methylation arrays. FHS used the Illumina HumanMethylation450 BeadChip (450K array; Illumina, Inc., CA, USA) and the other studies used the newer MethylationEPIC BeadChip (EPIC/850K array; Illumina, Inc.). We evaluated differentially methylated regions (DMRs) in relation to opioid use using the comb-p method implemented in the ENmix R package. EWAS meta-analysis results were used as the input. Comb-p combines irregularly spaced adjacent p-values to identify regions containing consecutive low p-values. Regional p-values were corrected for multiple testing using Šidák (default option). We set a threshold of Šidák P < 0.05 for significant genome-wide DMRs. Gene names used were those listed on the Illumina annotation. When no gene names were reported on the Illumina annotation, the authors used Zhou et al. and Homer v4.9.1 to annotate genes. P-values represented are the minimum p-values for the CpGs in the region. From supplementary table 5.

column6: Overlapping genes in opioid and nicotine addiction gene networks
column7: Overlapping opioid x nicotine genes
column9: With the goal of identifying commonly shared genes for alcohol, smoking and opioid addiction we conducted a literature search. We used the term “alcohol addiction SNPs” for alcohol addiction, “smoking/nicotine/tobacco addiction SNPs” for smoking addiction, and “opioid addiction SNPs” for opioid addiction. No limitations were placed on the year of publication. Non-human trials, literature reviews, and meta-analyses were excluded. Articles about treatment of drug addiction and drug addiction in patients with mental illnesses were also excluded. Ingenuity Pathway Analysis (IPA) was used to produce a comprehensive analysis of the genes commonly shared in these addiction pathways. The Core Analysis function was used to compare genes pooled from literature for each phenotype of addiction with the genes and other molecules in IPA’s database and generates gene networks based on their interactions. Gene networks were created for each addiction phenotype. Only the networks with a p-score of 5 or higher were considered significant (i.e., p-value ≤ 10−5), a nominal significance used in previous studies. The genes in each network were ranked based on number of edges, or interactions with other genes in the network. In order to identify the shared genes, the opioid addiction network was compared with alcohol and smoking addiction networks. From Table 4.

Only the networks with a p-score of 5 or higher were considered significant (i.e., p-value ≤ 10−5), a nominal significance used in previous studies. The genes in each network were ranked based on number of edges, or interactions with other genes in the network. In order to identify the shared genes, the opioid addiction network was compared with alcohol and smoking addiction networks. From Table 4.

column9: With the goal of identifying commonly shared genes for alcohol, smoking and opioid addiction we conducted a literature search. We used the term “alcohol addiction SNPs” for alcohol addiction, “smoking/nicotine/tobacco addiction SNPs” for smoking addiction, and “opioid addiction SNPs” for opioid addiction. No limitations were placed on the year of publication. Non-human trials, literature reviews, and meta-analyses were excluded. Articles about treatment of drug addiction and drug addiction in patients with mental illnesses were also excluded. column7: GWS SNPs OUD MVP_pvalue
column9: We conducted a cross-ancestry meta-analysis of opioid use disorder (OUD) that included African Americans (AA) (n=88,498), European Americans (EA) (n=302,585) and Hispanic Americans (HA) (n=34,861) participants recruited from the MVP that maximized OUD cases by using a less stringent definition (requiring the presence of a single OUD diagnostic code) and compared them to opioid-exposed controls (Ncases = 31,473 and Ncontrols = 394,471). OUD diagnostic codes based on ICD-9/10 were obtained from the VA EHRs. The main GWAS used a less stringent definition of OUD (N = 31,473), which required the presence of one inpatient or outpatient ICD-9/10 diagnostic code for OUD (304.0, 304.7, 305.5, F11.1, F11.2) in the EHR. MVP samples were genotyped with a custom Affymetrix Axiom Biobank Array. P-values are considered GWS after correction for multiple testing at p<5x10-8 (shown here). Nearest protein-coding gene containing the lead SNP or within the lead SNP (<=1Mb) shown. From supplementary table 3.

column6: Genes associated with differential ChIP-seq peaks from human dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (dlPFC) of controls vs opioid-overdose (OD) subjects_pvalue
column7: ChIP-seq assoc. genes from human dlPFC controls vs opioid OD_pvalue
column9:
"We identified opioid addiction–associated genes from multiple omics datasets using the postmortem dlPFC and GWAS single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)-nearest gene assignment (Figure 1). Using consistent significance thresholds within each omics dataset across studies (see Supplemental Methods), we identified 404 unique opioid addiction genes from the following omics data types: 256 genes associated with H3K27ac ChIP-seq peaks (shown here), 13 DNA methylation–associated genes, 33 GWAS-associated genes, 3 protein-coding genes associated with differentially abundant proteins from LC/MS proteomics, and 104 differentially expressed genes from RNA-seq. Differential ChIP-seq peaks were derived from previous H3K27ac ChIP-seq obtained from prefrontal cortex samples of 51 controls and 51 subjects that died of opioid overdose (Supplementary Table 1). From 388 differentially acetylated H3K27ac ChIP-seq peaks based on a threshold of Bonferroni-corrected p < 1e-7 , peaks were re-mapped to genes using the “annotatePeak” function from the ChIPseeker R function using hg19/GRCh37 coordinates and a 1kb window around the transcriptional start site to define the promoter region. This annotation was used in contrast to the GREAT peak-to-gene annotation listed in the original publication, as ChIP-seeker annotates the location of peaks (i.e. intronal, exonal, promoter, 5’ UTR, 3’UTR, distal intergenic) and mapped peaks to genes that GREAT did not annotate. Reciprocally, if ChIPseeker did not map a peak to a gene, the GREAT peak-to-gene annotation was used. Together, these 388 H3K27ac ChIP-seq peaks mapped to 267 unique genes. Furthermore, we included the 5 genes (ASTN2, DUSP4, ENOX1, GABBR2, KCNMA1) associated with variant enhancer loci lost in opioid overdose subjects using promoter-capture Hi-C that were statistically


significant at FDR-corrected p < 1e-6 , which added 2 unique genes to the H3K27ac ChIP-seq-associated gene list. Thirteen non-coding RNAs identified by ChIP-seq peaks were excluded from downstream analysis as they were not present in any layer of the multiplex network. Genes associated with H3K27ac ChIP-seq peaks are shown here (most significant associations shown, if multiple genes were associated with a peak). Data originally from Corradin et al., 2022 (PMID:35301427). From Supplementary Table 1. "

column9: We identified opioid addiction–associated genes from multiple omics datasets using the postmortem dlPFC and GWAS single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)-nearest gene assignment (Figure 1). Using consistent significance thresholds within each omics dataset across studies (see Supplemental Methods), we identified 404 unique opioid addiction genes from the following omics data types: 256 genes associated with H3K27ac ChIP-seq peaks, 13 DNA methylation–associated genes (shown here), 33 GWAS-associated genes, 3 protein-coding genes associated with differentially abundant proteins from LC/MS proteomics, and 104 differentially expressed genes from RNA-seq. Differentially methylated genes were identified using a previously published epigenome-wide association study of dlPFC tissue from 81 controls and 72 subjects who died from opioid overdose using the Illumina Infinium MethylationEPIC BeadChip DNA methylation chip testing 850,000 CpG regions (Supplementary Table 2). Thirteen CpG sites were identified with adjusted p-values of 0.4 (uncorrected p < 6e-6 ) and 3 CpG islands that were not mapped to genes (cg25084741, cg10759972, and cg26506680) were excluded. This yielded 13 unique genes identified by opioid-induced differential DNA methylation that were present in the multiplex network. Data originally from Shu et al., 2021 (PMID:﻿33667780). From Supplementary Table 2.

column7: GWAS human OUD (meta-analysis)_pvalue
column9: We identified opioid addiction–associated genes from multiple omics datasets using the postmortem dlPFC and GWAS single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)-nearest gene assignment (Figure 1). Using consistent significance thresholds within each omics dataset across studies (see Supplemental Methods), we identified 404 unique opioid addiction genes from the following omics data types: 256 genes associated with H3K27ac ChIP-seq peaks, 13 DNA methylation–associated genes, 33 GWAS-associated genes (shown here), 3 protein-coding genes associated with differentially abundant proteins from LC/MS proteomics, and 104 differentially expressed genes from RNA-seq. Genes were included from opioid use disorder, opioid addiction, and prescription opioid misuse GWAS based on SNP-nearest gene associations from SNPs with p < 5e-8 , resulting in 19 unique GWAS genes; an additional 15 unique genes were also identified from multi-trait analysis of GWAS (MTAG) using European opioid use disorder GWAS, alcohol use disorder, and cannabis use disorder summary statistics (Supplementary Table 3). These opioid addiction GWAS yielded 34 unique genes, but one gene (TMX2-CTNND1) was not present in any layer of the multiplex and was excluded from downstream analyses. In two studies, controls were exposed to opioids but did not develop opioid use disorder; in the other three studies, controls were a mixture of subjects exposed to opioids in their lifetime and unexposed individuals. Included GWAS from Kember et al., 2022 (PMID:36171425), Gaddis et al., 2022 (PMID:36207451), Deak et al., 2022 (PMID:35879402), Sanchez-Roige et al., 2021 (PMID:﻿34728798), and Zhou et al., 2020 (PMID:32492095). The most significant associations (pvalue) shown, if multiple genes were associated with a SNP. P-values and adjusted p-values appear to be the same value. From Supplementary Table 3.

column9: We identified opioid addiction–associated genes from multiple omics datasets using the postmortem dlPFC and GWAS single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP)-nearest gene assignment (Figure 1). Using consistent significance thresholds within each omics dataset across studies (see Supplemental Methods), we identified 404 unique opioid addiction genes from the following omics data types: 256 genes associated with H3K27ac ChIP-seq peaks, 13 DNA methylation–associated genes, 33 GWAS-associated genes, 3 protein-coding genes associated with differentially abundant proteins from LC/MS proteomics, and 104 differentially expressed genes from RNA-seq (shown here). Differentially expressed genes (DEGs; Supplementary Table 5) were identified from four RNA-seq studies derived from postmortem dlPFC of controls (n = 27, 14, 20, and 81, respectively) and subjects who died of opioid overdose or were diagnosed with opioid use disorder (n = 24, 27, 20, and 72, respectively) (1,10–12). DEGs were included in downstream analyses based on thresholds of adjusted p-value < 0.05 and |log2FC ≥ 0.5|. Using these thresholds, we identified 106 unique genes identified in opioid addiction by RNA-seq and 103 genes present in the multiplex were included in downstream analyses (AC018647.3, AL606753.2, and PARTICL were excluded). DEGs were included from Seney et al., 2021 (PMID:﻿34380600), Corradin et al., 2022 (PMID:35301427), Mendez et al., 2021 (PMID:34385598), and Sosnowski et al., 2022 (PMID:35301427). If a gene was listed multiple times (i.e. from multiple studies), the most significant p-value (and associated adjusted p-value and log2FC) was taken. From Supplementary Table 5.

column9:
Abstract
BACKGROUND:

Glucocorticoid hormones modulate acute and chronic behavioral and molecular responses to drugs of abuse including psychostimulants and opioids. There is growing evidence that glucocorticoids might also modulate behavioral responses to ethanol ( EtOH ). Acute EtOH activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, causing the release of adrenal glucocorticoid hormones. Our prior genomic studies suggest that glucocorticoids play a role in regulating gene expression in the prefrontal cortex (PFC) of DBA2/J (D2) mice following acute EtOH administration. However, few studies have analyzed the role of glucocorticoid signaling in behavioral responses to acute EtOH . Such work could be significant, given the predictive value for the level of response to acute EtOH in the risk for alcoholism.
METHODS:

We studied whether the glucocorticoid receptor (GR) antagonist, RU-486, or adrenalectomy (ADX) altered male D2 mouse behavioral responses to acute (locomotor activation, anxiolysis, or loss-of-righting reflex [LORR]) or repeated (sensitization) EtOH treatment. Whole-genome microarray analysis and bioinformatics approaches were used to identify PFC candidate genes possibly responsible for altered behavioral responses to EtOH following ADX.
RESULTS:

ADX and RU-486 both impaired acute EtOH (2 g/kg)-induced locomotor activation in D2 mice without affecting basal locomotor activity. However, neither ADX nor RU-486 altered the initiation of EtOH sensitization (locomotor activation or jump counts), EtOH -induced anxiolysis, or LORR. ADX mice showed microarray gene expression changes in PFC that significantly overlapped with acute EtOH -responsive gene sets derived by our prior microarray studies. Q-rtPCR analysis verified that ADX decreased PFC expression of Fkbp5 while significantly increasing Gpr6 expression. In addition, high-dose RU-486 pretreatment blunted EtOH -induced Fkbp5 expression.
CONCLUSIONS:

Our studies suggest that EtOH 's activation of adrenal glucocorticoid release and subsequent GR activation may partially modulate EtOH 's acute locomotor activation in male D2 mice. Furthermore, because adrenal glucocorticoid basal tone regulated PFC gene expression, including a significant set of acute EtOH -responsive genes, this suggests that glucocorticoid-regulated PFC gene expression may be an important factor modulating acute behavioral responses to EtOH .

Expression changes after extincition testing in a herion incubation model in the rat pre-frontal cortex
Rats were trained to self-administer heroin during 3 h daily ses- sions for 14 days. Following the self-administration period, rats were reintroduced to the self-administration chambers for a 90-min extinction session in which they could seek heroin, but received none.
Genome-wide analysis of gene expression in the medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) was conducted following the extinction session in rats that expressed behavioral incubation of heroin-seeking and goal-directed behavior.

column6: Common transcriptional effects in the mouse striatum following chronic treatment with heroin and methamphetamine
column7: HER & METH on Mouse Str
column9: Adult male C57BL/6J  mice were injected with heroin and methamphetamine (acutely and chronically, along with a control of saline) and then subsequently tested on locomotor activity, physical dependence and circadian rhythm activity. The mice were also tested after a withdrawal period of 1, 3, or 6 days. A microarray analysis was performed and 1508 probes were found to be significant with a P-value <0.05

column6: Assessment of individual differences in the rat nucleus accumbens transcriptome following taste-heroin extended access
column7: Rat NAc Transcriptome & Heroin
column9: Male Sprague-Dawley Rats were exposed to a saccharin solution for 5 minutes, then given the option to self-administer saccharin or heroin for 6 hours. This acted as a representation of the rat's "suppressing" ability. Following testing the rats were sacrificed and the nucleus accumbens was removed. Following RNA-seq an analysis was performed to identify significant and differentially expressed, transcripts with a read count of >1 on at least 100% of the samples in one group and with a fold change of > 1.25 were considered differentially expressed.

column6: Stress-related genes and heroin addiction: a role for a functional FKBP5 haplotype
column7: Stress & Heroin
column9: 1090 subjects were recruited for this study, 852 cases and 238 controls. All of the "case" subjects were former heroin addicts (used heroin multiple times/day for over a year). Blood samples were taken from the participants and genotyped for SNPs in 26 pre-determined genes known to be associated with stress. P value < 4.4E-4 was considered significant. SNP data not included
